title: Poitea dubia
intro: Poitea dubia, also known as the "Cordia" or "Wild Cherry," is a plant species native to the tropical regions of Central and South America, belonging to the family Boraginaceae. The leaves and bark of Poitea dubia have been traditionally used in folk medicine for their anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which have been linked to various health benefits, including wound healing, fever reduction, and the treatment of gastrointestinal issues. The bioactive constituents of Poitea dubia include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Poitea dubia is often prepared as a decoction, tea, or tincture, which are used to treat various ailments, including fever, cough, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Poitea dubia is a species of flowering plant in the legume family, Fabaceae. It is a climbing vine native to New Zealand. Poitea dubia is a member of the subfamily Faboideae. The plant is also known to be a part of the genus Poitea which is endemic to New Zealand. Poitea dubia is further classified under the kingdom Plantae.
